summary: My name Anoatubby is a warrior name within the Chickasaw Nation, meaning 'walk and kill.' It has deep roots, possibly going back to the 1700s and early 1800s, with a relative who was with Davy Crockett and worked alongside Andrew Jackson. The name connects me to my Chickasaw heritage and family history, including the Trail of Tears migration.

I was raised in the Chickasaw Nation, especially around Tishomingo, which was the original capital after the Chickasaws moved into Indian Territory in the late 1830s. My family has deep ties to the Nation, though I was somewhat estranged from the Chickasaw side after my father's early death. Over time, I grew interested in Chickasaw traditions, language, and culture, attending events like the Chickasaw Festival and learning about our history.

I have a family connection to Davy Crockett through a relative mentioned in his journal who worked with Andrew Jackson in the late 1700s and early 1800s. This link ties my family history to important figures in early American frontier history and the Chickasaw Nation's interactions during that time.

Andrew Jackson was a figure connected to my family's history, as a relative of mine worked with him alongside Davy Crockett in the late 1700s and early 1800s. This period was significant in the shaping of Chickasaw relations and the broader history of Indian Territory.

I was born in Denison, Texas, but moved away as a baby to Tishomingo, Oklahoma. My father worked for Kraft Foods in Denison before his tragic death in an industrial accident. Denison was the place of my birth and early family life before we returned to our Chickasaw roots in Oklahoma.

Tishomingo, Oklahoma, is where I was raised after moving there as a baby. It was the historic capital of the Chickasaw Nation after they relocated to Indian Territory in the late 1830s. The town is named after Chief Tishomingo, the last war chief of the Chickasaws, and it holds a central place in my family's and the Nation's history.

My father worked for Kraft Foods in Denison, Texas, where he was employed in manufacturing until he passed away in an industrial accident when I was very young. His work there was a significant part of our family's livelihood before we moved back to Tishomingo. Kraft Foods was connected to my early family life and my father's career.

The Chickasaw Nation moved into Indian Territory in the late 1830s, and Tishomingo became the central part of their new homeland. This relocation was part of the broader history of the Trail of Tears, which my family is connected to. Indian Territory represents the place where my ancestors resettled and where much of our cultural history is rooted.

Chief Tishomingo was the last war chief of the Chickasaw Nation, and the town of Tishomingo, Oklahoma, is named in his honor. His leadership marks an important part of Chickasaw history, and his legacy lives on in the place where I was raised. He symbolizes the warrior spirit and heritage of my people.

My mother, Opal Mitchell Anoatubby, was born in Greenville, Texas, before moving to Tishomingo as a young girl. Greenville was part of her early life and roots before she met my father and settled in Oklahoma. It represents the beginning of her journey before joining the Chickasaw community.

Mill Creek, Oklahoma, is where my father was born and grew up, a small town between Sulphur and Tishomingo. It was part of the Chickasaw area where my family lived and worked, including farming and labor jobs. Mill Creek connects me to my father's early life and Chickasaw heritage.

Sulphur, Oklahoma, is near Mill Creek where my father was born and raised. While I didn't live there directly, it is part of the region connected to my family's Chickasaw roots and history. The area around Sulphur and Tishomingo shaped much of my early understanding of where my people lived.

After my father's death, Social Security's Survivor's Insurance was a crucial support for my family. It helped provide financial stability while my mother worked hard to raise six children on her own. Social Security played an important role in helping us get through difficult times in Denison, Texas, and beyond.

Erv Mitchell was my maternal grandfather, a hardworking man who farmed and worked as a carpenter. I remember visiting his farm as a young boy, which was a great experience. He was part of a traditional family with strong values and had a close relationship with the Chickasaw people despite his Irish and Scotch-Irish heritage.

Bertha was my grandmother's twin sister, and they were both very feisty women with many stories about their youth. Their tales from the time before and after Oklahoma statehood in the early 20th century were vivid and memorable to me as a child. Bertha and my grandmother shared a strong bond that enriched my family's history.

Bullet Prairie is a small farming community near Tishomingo where my parents met and where my father's family had a homestead. My dad grew up between Mill Creek and Bullet Prairie, and it was an important place in my family's Chickasaw roots. Although small, it holds a significant place in our personal history.

My family traces its ancestry on my father's side back to the Trail of Tears, the forced relocation of the Chickasaw people in the 1830s. Though I was estranged from my Chickasaw roots growing up, this history is an important part of who I am. The Trail of Tears represents a painful but defining chapter in my family's story.

Chokma is the traditional Chickasaw greeting, and I remember my maternal grandfather saying it whenever I entered his home. Though he was of Irish descent, he had a close relationship with the Chickasaw people and used Chickasaw words around me. This greeting symbolizes the connection I felt to my Chickasaw heritage through family.

Pennington Creek runs near Tishomingo, and as a boy, my friends and I spent a lot of time there fishing and hunting. It was a place where Chickasaw and non-Indian children played together, and I learned some traditional skills and stories. Pennington Creek is part of my childhood memories and connection to the land.

The Chickasaw Festival in Tishomingo was an annual celebration where I was introduced to many aspects of our culture, including arts, crafts, and traditional foods like Pashofa. It was a way for me to connect with my heritage and learn more about the Chickasaw Nation as I grew older. The festival remains an important cultural event for our community.

Pashofa is a traditional Chickasaw dish made from pearl hominy cooked with pork. I first tasted it at the Chickasaw Festival and it left a lasting impression on me. This dish is a delicious part of our cultural heritage and a symbol of Chickasaw culinary tradition.

The Chickasaw language is part of the Muskogean language family, which we share with the Choctaw. Though I didn't grow up speaking it fluently, I learned some words from my grandfather and others around me. Understanding this language group helped me appreciate the close cultural ties between Chickasaw and Choctaw peoples.

The Choctaw Nation shares linguistic and cultural ties with the Chickasaw through the Muskogean languages. I had neighbors with Choctaw and Chickasaw heritage, and I learned about our shared Migration Legend involving two brothers guided by a leaning pole. The Choctaw are an important related tribe in understanding Chickasaw history and culture.

The Migration Legend tells of two brothers, ancestors of the Chickasaw and Choctaw, who were guided by a leaning pole as they traveled before settling in the southeastern United States. This story is part of our oral tradition and connects the Chickasaw and Choctaw peoples historically and culturally. It is a foundational legend I learned about as I explored my heritage.

While not directly mentioned in my personal story, the Mississippi River is part of the broader region where the Chickasaw and Choctaw peoples originally lived before their migration. It is a significant geographical landmark in the history of our ancestors' movements. Understanding this helps place our heritage in the larger context of southeastern Native American history.
